summary: How does a memory system come to KNOW one method/approach is better than another (earned comparative judgment) — oracle-free, from observation? Study the open question + mine the skill/procedural-memory cohort that attempts it.

cohort.system: SkillRL (arXiv 2602.08234, "Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning", Xia et al., UNC/UChicago/NEC, Feb 2026)
cohort.memory_shape: Two record types, both distilled by a teacher model (OpenAI o3) into a strict JSON schema. (1) SKILL (Tables 5/7): `{skill_id (e.g. "gen_001","dyn_001"), title (3-5 words, e.g. "Systematic Exploration"), principle (1-2 sentence actionable pattern, e.g. "Search every plausible surface exactly once before revisiting; prioritize unseen locations"), when_to_apply (applicability condition)}`. Split into General Skills Sg (universal, always injected) and Task-Specific Skills Sk (retrieved by embedding sim(ed,es)>τ, TopK). (2) FAILURE-LESSON (Table 6/8): `{err_id, failure_description, root_cause ("Why it happens"), mitigation ("How to avoid")}` — the distillation of a failed trajectory into "(1) point of failure, (2) flawed reasoning/action, (3) what should have been done, (4) general principle to prevent it" (§3.1). So the stored unit is a <situation, strategy, when-to-apply> skill or a <failure, root-cause, fix> counterfactual — genuinely method-knowledge, NOT a bare fact. But note: it stores "in situation X do Y" and "avoid failure Z", NOT an explicit ranked "strategy A beat strategy B" comparison.
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: It does NOT earn "A>B" by comparing two strategies head-to-head. It earns it in two oracle-bound moves. (1) SPLIT: every rollout trajectory gets a binary label from the environment — `r(τ)∈{0,1}`, "the binary task success indicator" (§3.1, l.152). Successful trajectories T+={τ:r=1} are distilled into "strategic patterns that led to task completion" (s+=MT(τ+,d)); failed T-={τ:r=0} into "failure lessons". "Better method" = whatever pattern the teacher extracts from the r=1 bucket vs the mitigation it writes for the r=0 bucket. The oracle decides the buckets; the teacher only writes prose. (2) REINFORCE via GRPO: the policy samples G trajectories, each gets binary reward Ri=r(τi)∈{0,1} (l.278), advantage Ai=(Ri−mean)/std over the group. Higher-reward trajectories (which used certain skills) get up-weighted. So "skill/strategy A is better than B" is expressed as A appearing in higher-advantage trajectories — a relative-reward signal, but the reward is still the benchmark's pass/fail. The library itself only GROWS (SKILLBANK←SKILLBANK∪Snew, l.252/268) — it never scores, ranks, or prunes skills; the ranking lives implicitly in the policy weights, not in memory. Evolution is triggered when held-out `Acc(C)<β` (benchmark success rate per category, l.225-228).
cohort.updates_over_time: Yes — this is its headline "recursive evolution": after each validation epoch, categories with Acc(C)<β get NEW skills distilled from freshly-failed validation trajectories (Snew=MT(Tval−, SKILLBANK)); the teacher is prompted to "(1) identify failure patterns not addressed by current skills, (2) propose new skills, (3) suggest refinements to existing skills that proved ineffective". Library grows 55→100 skills over 150 steps (Fig 3). BUT the loop is monotone-additive: it only ADDS/refines skills for still-failing categories; there is no down-ranking, decay, forgetting, or A-vs-B replacement of a skill by a better one. "Proved ineffective" is judged solely by the category still being below the benchmark threshold, not by any per-skill outcome attribution. So it's a real learning loop, but the revision signal is the oracle's success rate, not observed per-skill utility.
cohort.the_hard_part: The whole edifice rests on a free, automatic, ground-truth `r(τ)∈{0,1}` from a simulator (ALFWorld/WebShop win-condition; QA exact-match against gold answers). That IS the "did method A work better than B" oracle — remove it and SkillRL has no way to bucket trajectories into success/failure, no GRPO advantage, no Acc(C) evolution trigger. Nothing in the earned judgment is oracle-free: the teacher model (o3) never JUDGES which trajectory was better — it is handed pre-labeled "SUCCESSFUL TRAJECTORIES / FAILED TRAJECTORIES" buckets (Prompt B.2) and only writes the skill prose. What it papers over: (a) it never actually compares two competing strategies for the same situation and picks a winner — "better" is laundered through policy-weight up-weighting, so the memory store holds no explicit A>B record you could cite; (b) "refinements to skills that proved ineffective" implies per-skill credit assignment, but the only signal is the whole category's success rate, so it cannot tell WHICH skill in the injected set of ~6 caused the win or loss (no counterfactual per-skill attribution); (c) it is monotone-additive — a skill once written is never demoted even if later evidence contradicts it. For cmk this is oracle-BOUND and NOT transferable: cmk has no benchmark win-condition at conversation time, so the exact mechanism (binary reward → GRPO advantage → success-rate-triggered distillation) cannot run. The ONLY oracle-free-ish idea to borrow is the STRUCTURAL one — the <situation,strategy,when-to-apply> skill schema and the <failure,root-cause,mitigation> counterfactual schema are worth stealing as a fact shape; but SkillRL's answer to "how did you KNOW this strategy is better" is, honestly, "the benchmark told us." It is a clean example of the oracle-bound case, not the oracle-free earned-judgment cmk is chasing.
cohort.quote: "we deliberately preserve both successful trajectories T+ = {τi : r(τi) = 1} and failed trajectories T− = {τi : r(τi) = 0}, where r(·) denotes the binary task success indicator." (§3.1) || "Each trajectory τ(i) receives a binary reward Ri = r(τ(i)) ∈ {0, 1} indicating task successfulness. The normalized advantage ... Ai = (Ri − mean({Rj}))/std({Rj})." (§3.3) || "After each validation epoch, we monitor the success rate Acc(C) for each task category C. To ensure targeted growth, the evolution is triggered only for categories where Acc(C) < β." (§3.3) || Prompt B.2: "Analyze these successful and failed trajectories ... SUCCESSFUL TRAJECTORIES: {success patterns} FAILED TRAJECTORIES: {failure patterns} Generate 8-12 GENERAL SKILLS" — teacher receives pre-labeled buckets. || Skill schema: "Each skill s ∈ SKILLBANK is structured with: a concise name ..., a principle describing the strategy, and when to apply conditions." (§3.2)

cohort.system: SkillRevise (arXiv 2606.01139) — execution-grounded iterative revision of agent skills (markdown procedural docs) from execution evidence + a fixed repair-principle memory.
cohort.memory_shape: Two stores. (1) The SKILL itself is a markdown procedural doc: "# <Skill Name>, Purpose, When to Use, Procedure, Constraints/Pitfalls" — the revised skill IS the stored method-judgment ("do it this way, not the old way"). (2) The Principle Memory entry (Table 9 schema, verbatim fields): <trigger, defect label, repair rule, action template, verification template, transfer constraint, evidence> — a reusable comparative repair-judgment ("when defect D appears, apply repair R instead"). Initialized with 7 PRE-AUTHORED principles; optional absorption of new ones requires they be "evidence-backed, skill-level, utility-improving, and transferable."
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: RE-VALIDATION BY RE-EXECUTION AGAINST A TASK VERIFIER. Per round i, executing skill S_i yields e_i=(τ_i, v_i, r_i, c_i)=Φ(T,S_i,π_θ): τ_i trajectory, v_i "verifier feedback or evaluation evidence", r_i "the outcome score or pass/fail reward", c_i costs. Diagnosis D_i=(V_i,A_i,K_i) builds a verification spec V_i ("output paths, schemas, formats, terminal sentinels, and pass/fail assertions"); a repair principle is retrieved from Principle Memory, bound, and applied via a Revision Operator; the candidate is RE-EXECUTED. Across the revision budget (=3 per benchmark), it keeps the best observed: S*_≤B = argmax_{S∈H_≤B} U(S,T), with utility U = α·Δ_succ + β·g_succ·Δ_eff + γ·Δ_trans − λ·C_intf (Eq.9), g_succ=1[succ(S,T)=1]. The abstract's "retains the first verifier-passing skill, falls back to empirical utility only when no candidate succeeds" is this same rule: success dominates utility, empirical utility (efficiency/transfer terms) only tiebreaks/fallbacks. So it earns "A>B" by literally running both and seeing which passes the task's pass/fail assertions.
cohort.updates_over_time: Real learning loop WITHIN an episode (iterative diagnose→edit→re-run over the revision budget, keeping argmax-utility). Cross-episode, the Principle Memory can OPTIONALLY absorb new evidence-backed principles — but this is described as optional and the core 7 principles are pre-authored/fixed during benchmark eval, so the comparative-judgment revision is mostly one-shot-per-task-refined, not a long-horizon self-revising principle base. The skill itself is revised (updated), but the "which repair beats which" principle knowledge is largely frozen.
cohort.the_hard_part: The whole method rests on a re-runnable task with a benchmark-provided pass/fail evaluator — the exact oracle cmk lacks at conversation time. It earns "A>B" honestly (it re-runs and checks) but ONLY because succ(S,T) is a ground-truth test. Even the "empirical utility fallback" is oracle-gated: Δ_succ IS the benchmark success delta and g_succ=1[succ=1] gates the efficiency credit, so there is no purely-observational comparison anywhere — no path compares two skills without the task evaluator. The authors make NO oracle-free claim and explicitly warn (App. E): "its revisions are only as informative as the available feedback" and "sparse tests, opaque scoring scripts, or proxy checks may cause the diagnosis module to repair the wrong behavior, overfit to visible assertions." What it papers over for cmk's question: it assumes the answer key exists and is cheap to re-consult — precisely cmk's missing ingredient. Transferable to cmk = the SHAPE only (diagnose-defect → retrieve-repair-principle → edit → re-validate; and the principle-memory schema trigger/defect/repair/verification-template/evidence). NOT transferable = the re-execute-and-check-succ retention gate. Honest verdict: SkillRevise is oracle-BOUND earned judgment, not evidence that oracle-free comparative judgment is achievable.
cohort.quote: "By re-executing candidates, it retains the first verifier-passing skill within the revision budget and falls back to empirical utility only when no candidate succeeds." (abstract) | "execution of the current skill produces e_i=(τ_i,v_i,r_i,c_i)=Φ(T,S_i,π_θ), where τ_i is the trajectory, v_i is verifier feedback or evaluation evidence, r_i is the outcome score or pass/fail reward, and c_i records costs" (§3.4) | "The verification specification V_i describes the observable requirements against which the run is judged, including output paths, schemas, formats, terminal sentinels, and pass/fail assertions." (§3.1) | "U(S,T)=α·Δ_succ(S,T)+β·g_succ(S,T)·Δ_eff(S,T)+γ·Δ_trans(S,ℱ)−λ·C_intf(S,ℱ)" with "g_succ=1[succ(S,T)=1]" (Eq.9) | "our current implementation mainly uses benchmark-provided verifiers and task traces" (App. E) | "SkillRevise improves skills by reading execution traces and verifier-facing failures, so its revisions are only as informative as the available feedback" / "sparse tests, opaque scoring scripts, or proxy checks may cause the diagnosis module to repair the wrong behavior, overfit to visible assertions" (App. E) | Principle Memory schema (Table 9): "trigger, defect label, repair rule, action template, verification template, transfer constraint, evidence"

cohort.system: SkillAdaptor: Self-Adapting Skills for LLM Agents from Trajectories (arXiv 2606.01311v1)
cohort.memory_shape: A skill is a textual record with strict fields: `skill_profile{title, principle, when_to_apply, procedure (3-6 deterministic steps), qualification_criteria, negative_example{what_not_to_do, why_it_fails}}`. The `negative_example{what_not_to_do, why_it_fails}` field is the closest thing to a stored comparative judgment — it encodes "don't do X because it fails" — but the paper gives NO algorithm for how it's populated (generator prompt merely says "Include explicit negative examples and stop conditions"). Attribution artifacts are transient, not stored: Localize(q,τ,Sq)→(t*,π) yields the earliest fault step; Link(q,τ,t*,Sq)→{(sj,wj)} a "weighted suspect set over retrieved skills, where a larger wj indicates stronger estimated responsibility." No <win,lose,dimension> tuple or ranked A-vs-B record persists — only the revised/regenerated skill survives.
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: Two-layer split. (1) ATTRIBUTION layer (oracle-free reasoning): given a FAILED trajectory, the Localizer LLM predicts "the earliest accountable fault step whose correction is expected to improve the final outcome"; the Linker LLM assigns responsibility weights to retrieved skills and routes to `revise` (skill_wrong) or `generate` (skill_missing). This layer reads ONLY the trajectory + a failure bit — no gold answer as input — so the "which step is at fault" guess is inferred, not oracle-supplied. (2) ACCEPTANCE layer (oracle-bound counterfactual re-run): "the framework re-executes tasks under both settings and compares their outcomes: Δ = 𝔼[ℳ(q;K+)] − 𝔼[ℳ(q;K)]. The candidate update is accepted only when Δ ≥ 0." So "revised skill K+ is BETTER than K" is EARNED by counterfactually re-running the agent and scoring against ℳ, the "benchmark-defined success indicator." Attribution proposes the edit; the benchmark oracle disposes of it via a before/after re-run.
cohort.updates_over_time: Yes, iteratively — but each accepted revision is gated one-shot by the Δ≥0 re-run, not by accumulating evidence over episodes. Skills are bootstrapped from successful reference trajectories ("Successful trajectories are archived as reference traces, from which initial skills are distilled to construct K₀"), then revised/regenerated per failure. There is no revision of a stored comparative JUDGMENT as more evidence arrives; there's revision of the skill TEXT, each committed only if the immediate counterfactual re-run doesn't regress benchmark score. The learning loop is real but its truth-arbiter is the grader, re-consulted every acceptance.
cohort.transferable_to_cmk: partial. The oracle-free HALF transfers cleanly: cmk could run a Localizer/Linker-style LLM pass over a failed session to attribute "which prior stored method/step misled us" and mint a `negative_example{what_not_to_do, why_it_fails}` markdown record — pure trajectory-reading, no grader needed, and it maps directly onto cmk's existing dead-end-veto / negative-exemplar-retention signals. What does NOT transfer is the part that makes SkillAdaptor's judgment EARNED rather than merely hypothesized: the Δ≥0 acceptance gate needs a re-runnable task with a benchmark success indicator. cmk has no answer key and (usually) can't counterfactually re-run a past conversation, so it cannot verify "the revised method actually beats the old one" — it can only PROPOSE the contrast (attribution) and hope, or wait for the same situation to recur and cheaply A/B across episodes (which SkillAdaptor does NOT do — it re-runs synchronously against the oracle). So cmk can adopt the attribution + negative-example SHAPE, but the comparative judgment stays UNVERIFIED without the oracle SkillAdaptor leans on.
cohort.the_hard_part: The honest crux: SkillAdaptor's fine-grained attribution does NOT itself earn "A>B" — it only LOCALIZES a suspect. The Linker's weights wj are "estimated responsibility," an LLM guess with no verification; the paper offers zero before/after test that the localized step was truly the fault. The word "better" is entirely carried by the Qualification stage's Δ≥0 re-run against the benchmark oracle. Strip the grader and you keep the plausible attribution but LOSE the earned comparison — you'd be committing skill edits on an unvalidated LLM hunch about blame. The paper papers over three things: (i) it never algorithmically defines success()/ℳ ("benchmark-defined success indicator," source undefined) — the oracle is assumed into existence; (ii) it never states whether Δ is measured on the same failed task (overfitting risk) or held-out tasks — "re-executes tasks" with an 𝔼[·] but no task set specified; (iii) the whole loop bootstraps from labeled successful trajectories and cannot start from zero-success. Net: the ATTRIBUTION is oracle-free and transferable; the JUDGMENT that the fix is an improvement is oracle-BOUND and is NOT transferable to cmk's no-answer-key setting. For cmk, this is a cautionary datapoint — fine-grained blame attribution is cheap and doable offline, but calling the result "better" without a re-run-and-score oracle is exactly the unearned judgment the maintainer is worried about.
cohort.quote: "the framework re-executes tasks under both settings and compares their outcomes: Δ = 𝔼[ℳ(q;K+)] − 𝔼[ℳ(q;K)]. The candidate update is accepted only when Δ ≥ 0. Otherwise, the modification is discarded and the original collection K is retained unchanged." | "success(⋅) is the benchmark-defined success indicator." | "the Localizer predicts the accountable failure step ... t* denotes the earliest accountable fault step whose correction is expected to improve the final outcome" | "{(sj,wj)}=Link(q,τ,t*,Sq) ... a weighted suspect set over retrieved skills, where a larger wj indicates stronger estimated responsibility for the failure."

cohort.system: MUSE — "Learning on the Job: An Experience-Driven Self-Evolving Agent for Long-Horizon Tasks" (Yang et al., arXiv 2510.08002). Plan-Execute-Reflect-Memorize loop over a 3-tier natural-language Memory Module (Strategic / Procedural-SOP / Tool), evaluated on TheAgentCompany (TAC) benchmark.
cohort.memory_shape: Three natural-language tiers, quoted verbatim. (1) Strategic Memory M_strat: "<Dilemma, Strategy> key-value pairs" — problem→solution lessons "from challenges that require multiple attempts to overcome" (Table 6 renders them as <Principle, Description>, e.g. "Systemic Root Cause: Diagnose and address underlying systemic causes of recurring errors..."). Post-task it also extracts "<Dilemma, Resolution Pattern> pairs." (2) Procedural Memory M_proc: SOPs as "p = (index_p, content_p)", index-then-content split; index "documents the key analyses, precaution, core parameters, and operational steps for each sub-task"; hierarchical (app → SOP). Table 7 shape ≈ <Application, Function, {Preconditions, Steps, Notes}>. (3) Tool Memory M_tool = {D_static, I_dynamic} (static description + dynamic per-observation instruction). NOTE: there is NO <win,lose,dimension> triple and NO stored failed-trajectory — a failure yields only an ephemeral diagnostic R_fail fed back to the PE Agent for replanning, never a persisted negative exemplar.
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: It does NOT earn "A>B" by any comparison — this is the honest crux. The unit of learning is a per-sub-task binary success/failure flag f from the Reflect Agent (an independent LLM supervisor), decided by a self-verification checklist — "1) Truthfulness Verification 2) Deliverable Verification 3) Data Fidelity" — executed via "trajectory referencing" (trace conclusions back to observations o_t) + "active verification" (re-run tools against the live environment E to cross-check). On f=success it "summarizes the effective operational sequence from h_k:t into a new SOP p_new"; on f=failure it writes a diagnostic and the PE Agent replans. So memory grows by SUCCESS-GATED ACCUMULATION, not by ranking two candidate methods. Old-vs-new reconciliation is a post-task LLM-judge pass: "all three types of memory undergo a thorough refinement and integration process, aiming to integrate new and old knowledge, eliminate redundancy, and generalize common patterns" — a merge/dedup/generalize step with NO explicit better-than test, NO A/B re-run, NO keep-the-winner. Implicit "better" leaks in only via the retry rule (on a stuck sub-task the retry is "no longer required to use Procedural Memory... to discover novel methods when existing knowledge is erroneous") and via each SOP's own Notes ("Python file handling is more robust than shell echo due to escaping issues") — but that comparative note is authored by the reflection LLM from a single trajectory, not measured across A/B episodes.
cohort.updates_over_time: Two-stage, and it is ACCUMULATE-then-DISTILL, not a revising A>B loop. Stage 1 (immediate): "immediately following a successful sub-task, the Reflect Agent dynamically adds the new SOP p_new to M_proc for immediate reuse." Stage 2 (post-task global refinement): "deduplication, generalization" + "integrate new and old knowledge, eliminate redundancy, and generalize common patterns" — an LLM-judge consolidation that can overwrite/merge an old SOP into a generalized one. Memory is "updated, merged, and refined after each task, always maintaining a concise size," so it is bounded/compacted rather than append-forever — but supersession is by LLM-judged redundancy, NOT by a scored contest between old and new. There is NO per-item usefulness counter, NO re-scoring of a stored SOP after later episodes, NO prune-on-later-failure. So the judgment is one-shot distilled at task-end and then only re-touched if a future refinement pass happens to merge it — a real cross-episode accumulation loop, but a weak learning loop for comparative method-knowledge. Crucially, "learn by contrasting successful and failed trajectories" is stated as FUTURE work ("Looking ahead, we envision..."), confirming MUSE does not yet do contrastive/comparative earning.
cohort.transferable_to_cmk: partial — and it is the closest-to-cmk of the oracle-free crowd (natural-language markdown-ish memory, single environment, no fine-tune, no benchmark reward at learning time, human-optional). Directly liftable: (a) the <Dilemma, Strategy> shape maps onto a cmk "lesson" fact — key=the recurring bind you hit, value=the resolution + a one-line Notes/why; (b) the index/content split (load lightweight SOP index at session start, fetch full body on demand) is exactly cmk's bounded-hot-index-vs-deep-archive design; (c) the SOP "Notes" field IS a stored comparative judgment ("X is more robust than Y because Z") that a single-user markdown kit can capture verbatim, sourced from one observed trajectory. NOT transferable as an "earned A>B": MUSE's better-than is authored by an LLM reflecting on ONE success, not measured across episodes — so adopting it gives cmk asserted method-notes, not earned rankings. To get true earning cmk would still need cross-episode A/B (recurrence + used-vs-ignored + later-failure re-scoring), which MUSE explicitly defers.
cohort.the_hard_part: The honest crux: MUSE gets to be oracle-FREE precisely BECAUSE it never actually earns "A better than B" — it substitutes a cheaper question. Its self-verification (Reflect Agent) answers "did THIS attempt satisfy its own goal_i checklist?" (truthfulness/deliverable/data-fidelity), which is a per-episode PASS/FAIL, not a between-method COMPARISON. It papers over the comparison entirely: (1) failed methods are never stored as negative exemplars — a failure becomes an ephemeral R_fail diagnostic and evaporates, so there is no A-vs-B pair left in memory to compare; (2) old-vs-new SOP supersession is an LLM-judge "dedup/generalize" merge with no scored contest — the model DECLARES the generalization, nobody measures that the merged SOP outperforms either parent; (3) the "better" inside an SOP's Notes is the reflection LLM's single-trajectory opinion, i.e. an ungrounded self-report, exactly the dedup-self-poisoning risk cmk already flags. It also quietly leans on a self-verifiable environment (it can re-run browser/shell/code tools to check its own work) — that closed-loop verifiability is what lets the LLM-judge stand in for an oracle; a domain where you can't cheaply re-verify (subjective/creative work) would collapse the whole signal. And the paper's OWN limitation section concedes the benchmark's checkpoint oracle sometimes "incorrectly penalized" plausible strategies — i.e. even the external score it uses only for evaluation is not a clean better-than oracle. Net: MUSE is strong evidence that oracle-free SELF-VERIFIED ACCUMULATION works to raise competence, and honest evidence that it is NOT the same thing as earned comparative method-judgment — which MUSE itself lists as future work.
cohort.quote: "if f=success, it summarizes the effective operational sequence from h_k:t into a new SOP p_new for the Procedural Memory M_proc; if f=failure, it generates a failure cause analysis report R_fail." // "immediately following a successful sub-task, the Reflect Agent dynamically adds the new SOP p_new to M_proc for immediate reuse. Second, after the entire task is complete, the agent performs a higher-level, global refinement (e.g., deduplication, generalization)." // "all three types of memory undergo a thorough refinement and integration process, aiming to integrate new and old knowledge, eliminate redundancy, and generalize common patterns within M." // On the completion signal being oracle-free: the Reflect Agent "conducts an autonomous assessment based on its environmental observations and the PE Agent's sub-task execution trajectory... requiring no human intervention" and "outputs a success/failure flag f." // Benchmark score used ONLY for external evaluation, not learning: "For evaluation, we rely on the official protocol provided by the TAC benchmark... S_partial = 0.5·Completed_ckpt/Total_ckpt + 0.5·S_full." // Comparative learning explicitly deferred: "Looking ahead, we envision an ideal agent accumulating extensive experience through long-term practice and learn by contrasting successful and failed trajectories."

cohort.system: AlphaOPT — self-improving optimization experience library (arXiv 2510.18428v4, Kong et al.). Focus: how it earns "solving-strategy A beats B" and whether that earning is oracle-free.
cohort.memory_shape: An "insight" is a structured 4-tuple (verbatim, p.343-348): "Taxonomy, hierarchical labels for indexing and retrieval; Condition, an explicit description of the applicable prerequisites and triggering signals in the problem; Explanation, the underlying principle of applying this insight; and Example, a concrete demonstration such as corresponding mathematical expressions or code snippet." This is genuine comparative method-knowledge, NOT a fact: Explanation = "the underlying principle of applying this insight" (a reusable modeling strategy — e.g. "use Big-M linking when service is allowed only if a facility is opened"), and Condition = the earned applicability boundary (WHEN this strategy beats not-using-it). Alongside each insight the system maintains a "performance profile" — a running partition Π(i)={Positive S+, Negative S-, Unretrieved S^u} of every task the insight touched: S+ "contributed to the correct formulation", S- "was misleading and degraded performance", S^u "was not retrieved but would have been beneficial." So the stored judgment shape is <strategy(Explanation), when-it-applies(Condition), tasks-it-helped / tasks-it-hurt / tasks-it-should-have-helped>.
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: TWO loops, both anchored to a solver + ground-truth answer. (1) Library Learning earns "this insight beats not-having-it": extraction only fires on a FAILURE ("If the generated program does not achieve the correct optimal value…"), then solver-guided self-exploration iterates programs "reus[ing] prior failures as context" until one "achieves its correct objective," treated "as a proxy for the gold standard" for extracting the anchor insight — so the very existence of an insight is a success-vs-fail contrast, minted only after a solver confirms the objective. New insights pass "Execution verification: reapply the new insight… to verify that the new insight can resolve the original failure and lead to optimality." (2) Library Evolution earns "Condition version A beats version B": a diagnostic LLM compares "the formulation and program generated under the guidance of retrieved insights with the ground truth to locate discrepancies," partitions tasks into Positive/Negative/Unretrieved, proposes multiple candidate refined Conditions (add preconditions, keyword anchors, merge triggers), tests each over R_i = S+ ∪ S- ∪ S^u, scores p_i = (|kept positives| + |corrected negatives| + |recovered unretrieved|)/|R_i|, and "accept[s] refinements that increase p_i and keep[s] the one with the highest p_i." That p_i is a REAL cross-episode A/B: it aggregates evidence across many tasks and picks the condition-boundary that maximizes net correct retrievals. But every term in it (positive/negative/corrected/recovered) is defined by whether the resulting program hit the ground-truth optimal value.
cohort.updates_over_time: Yes — genuinely iterative, not one-shot-frozen. The judgment REVISES: "By maintaining these partitions across iterations, the system continuously builds a performance profile for each insight," and Conditions are refined "at the end of each iteration" toward "the correct retrieval boundary." An insight can be pruned, its Condition strengthened (Negative tasks "contribute explicit inapplicability clauses") or broadened (Unretrieved tasks "highlight missing applicability signals"), and insights get merged for redundancy. There's even a causal-attribution step: "If a failed task is subsequently solved after removing a misleading (negative) insight or by injecting a previously unretrieved one, the system attributes the failure to condition misalignment rather than lack of knowledge" — a counterfactual re-run that decides WHY it failed (bad routing vs missing method) and avoids minting a redundant insight. This is a real learning loop; the library has "bounded growth."
cohort.transferable_to_cmk: partial. The DATA SHAPES transfer cleanly and are the most valuable takeaway for cmk: (a) split a stored strategy into Explanation (the reusable principle) + Condition (an explicit, revisable applicability boundary) — cmk today stores flat facts with no "when does this apply" field; adding an earned/refinable Condition is the single most portable idea. (b) attach a per-insight Positive/Negative/Unretrieved tally so a strategy carries its own track record, and REVISE the Condition when the tally shows over/under-firing (too-broad = misretrieval, too-narrow = missed). (c) the causal-attribution trick (removing a memory fixed the outcome ⇒ blame routing, not knowledge) maps directly onto cmk's used-vs-ignored / recall-miss signals. What does NOT transfer is the ENGINE that fills those tallies: every Positive/Negative label is decided by a solver checking the program against a ground-truth optimal value. cmk has no such checkable answer at conversation time. So cmk can adopt AlphaOPT's memory SCHEMA and revision LOOP, but must substitute its oracle-free signal portfolio (user-correction, cmk-forget, contradiction, used-vs-ignored, recurrence) for the p_i numerator — and accept those signals are noisier and rarer than a solver verdict. AlphaOPT is proof the schema+loop works; it is NOT proof it works without an oracle.
cohort.the_hard_part: The honest crux: AlphaOPT's "A>B" is fully oracle-BOUND and NOT transferable as an oracle-free result. It advertises "limited supervision… answer-only feedback without gold-standard programs," and that framing is real but narrow — it drops the gold PROGRAM, never the gold ANSWER. The evaluation protocol is explicit (verbatim, p.644): "a task is considered successful if the LLM-generated optimal value closely aligns with the provided ground-truth solution." Every atom the learning loop runs on — Success(t|ℓ), the Positive/Negative/Unretrieved partition, the p_i score, the "resolve the original failure and lead to optimality" verification gate, the "proxy for the gold standard" self-explored anchor — bottoms out in "did the program reach the provided ground-truth optimal value?" Remove that answer key and the entire comparative machinery has nothing to label tasks with. What it papers over for a memory system like cmk: it earns crisp method-judgment ONLY because optimization has a cheap, automatic, binary verifier (run the solver, compare one number to the answer key). That is the ideal case — a dense, unambiguous, oracle-checkable outcome per episode. It says NOTHING about how to earn "A>B" when the outcome is unverifiable and there is no answer to check against — which is exactly cmk's regime (open-ended conversation, no ground-truth, no solver). AlphaOPT is a clean, well-engineered demonstration that IF you have an oracle, the right memory shape is <principle, revisable-condition, cross-task success/fail tally> and the right loop is diagnosis→refine→keep-highest-p_i. It is not evidence that the earning can be done oracle-free — it is the boundary marker showing what you lose when the oracle is gone. It even confirms the oracle is doing the work, not the LLM: the self-explored "proxy gold" can be spuriously correct (footnote 2: an incorrect program could pass by hitting the right value; they audited it to 0.8%, 3 cases) — so even their ONE concession toward oracle-freedom (self-explored anchors instead of gold programs) is only trustworthy because a second oracle check (the answer value) bounds the error. No answer key, no bound.
cohort.quote: "a task is considered successful if the LLM-generated optimal value closely aligns with the provided ground-truth solution" (p.644-645). // Insight schema (p.343): "Each insight is represented as a structured 4-tuple: Taxonomy… Condition, an explicit description of the applicable prerequisites and triggering signals… Explanation, the underlying principle of applying this insight; and Example." // Learning trigger (p.337): "If the generated program does not achieve the correct optimal value, the system performs solver-guided self-exploration… Once a program achieves its correct objective, it is treated as a proxy for the gold standard in anchor insight extraction." // Comparative metric (p.462-468): "p_i = (|kept positives| + |corrected negatives| + |recovered unretrieved|) / |R_i|… We accept refinements that increase p_i and keep the one with the highest p_i." // Partition (p.427): "Π(i)={Positive: S+_i, Negative: S−_i, Unretrieved: S^u_i}" where S+ "contributed to the correct formulation," S− "was misleading and degraded performance," S^u "was not retrieved but would have been beneficial." // Oracle caveat (footnote 2): "an incorrect program could in principle pass by reaching the correct optimal value… we found such spurious passes negligible (3 cases, 0.8%…)."

cohort.system: Agent Workflow Memory (AWM) — Wang, Mao, Fried, Neubig (CMU/MIT), arXiv 2409.07429, ICML 2025. Repo: github.com/zorazrw/agent-workflow-memory
cohort.memory_shape: A workflow = plain-text routine, NOT a comparative judgment. Paper schema (§3): "a textual description of the workflow d; and second, a series of steps to finish the workflow (p1,p2,...)" where each step p has three parts: NL environment description, reasoning process, executable action. Stored as flat concatenated text in workflow/<website>.txt and prepended to the agent prompt. There is NO field for "better-than", no dimension, no win/lose, no score, no counterpart-B. The induction instruction's only quality clause is de-duplication: "Do not generate similar or overlapping workflows. Each workflow should be a commonly-reused sub-routine of the tasks. Each workflow should have at least two steps." So the memory unit is "here is a reusable sub-routine distilled from a successful trajectory" — a distilled PROCEDURE, not a judgment "procedure A beat procedure B."
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: It does NOT earn "A > B" — this is the honest headline. AWM's only comparative act is binary success-vs-not on a WHOLE TRAJECTORY, and that verdict is SUPPLIED, not induced from comparison. Two gates in the code (induce_rule.py / induce_prompt.py), selected by a `criteria` flag: (1) OFFLINE `criteria='gt'` — gates on WebArena's built-in ground-truth reward: `if json.load(open(.../summary_info.json))["cum_reward"]` (non-zero reward → keep). (2) ONLINE `criteria='autoeval'` — gates on `if record[0]["rm"]:`, the binary label from a separate LM-based evaluator: paper §"We adopt the LM-based evaluation model of Pan et al. (2024) to output a binary label, L_eval(e^t) ∈ {0,1}, that judges if e^t successfully solves q^t... If e^t is predicted as success, i.e., 1, we then transform it into workflow(s)." Then: successful trajectories → template-dedup by `intent_template_id` → dedup by abstract trajectory (action-fn names + primary args) → `random_group_sample(..., 1)` picks ONE PER GROUP AT RANDOM. There is NO ranking, NO scoring, NO pairwise preference, NO A-vs-B anywhere. It learns "this succeeded, so distill its routine into memory" — reinforce-on-success ONLY. It never observes B, never compares A to B, never revises which routine is preferred.
cohort.updates_over_time: Partially — online AWM accretes workflows episode-by-episode (each newly-successful test trajectory is induced and appended to memory mid-run, so the memory grows across the run and later tasks see earlier-task workflows). BUT it is one-directional ACCRETION, not REVISION: a workflow, once added, is never re-scored, down-weighted, compared against a rival, or pruned. There is no learning loop that says "workflow A used to be preferred, but evidence now favors B." No negative/failure trajectory ever enters memory (only label==1). No forgetting, no consolidation of competing routines. So "updates over time" = the corpus grows; the JUDGMENT (there is none beyond binary keep/discard) does not refine.
cohort.transferable_to_cmk: partial — and the honest read is "the shape transfers, the earning does not solve cmk's problem." What transfers cleanly: (a) the DISTILL-A-REUSABLE-ROUTINE-FROM-A-GOOD-EPISODE move is exactly a markdown-fact kit's sweet spot — an LLM reads a transcript, extracts a named multi-step procedure, writes it as a fact file; no infra needed. (b) The reinforce-on-success asymmetry (only successful runs get distilled) is trivially implementable. What does NOT transfer / does not answer the deep question: AWM never earns "A>B" — so adopting it gives cmk BETTER FACTS (reusable procedures instead of atomic prefs), NOT earned method-judgment. And its success signal is either a benchmark oracle (`cum_reward`, unavailable to cmk at conversation time) or an LLM-judge (Pan et al. 2024 autoeval) — cmk could run the LLM-judge route oracle-free, but that only classifies whole-trajectory success/fail; it still never COMPARES two rival routines for the same goal. cmk would have to add the comparison layer AWM lacks (e.g. keep two candidate routines for the same recurring task, judge outcomes across episodes) — AWM provides zero mechanism for that.
cohort.the_hard_part: The crux AWM papers over: it collapses "is this trajectory good?" into a SUPPLIED binary label and never confronts "is routine A better than routine B FOR THE SAME GOAL?" — the actual comparative question. It dodges the hard part three ways. (1) It leans on the environment: WebArena/Mind2Web HAVE a `cum_reward` oracle, so offline mode never has to earn success — it's told. (2) When it drops the oracle (online), it swaps in ANOTHER model's judgment (the Pan-2024 LM evaluator) — the success call is still delegated, just to an LLM instead of an answer key; the paper's own reported 24.6%/51.1% gains use this, but the offline SOTA numbers ride the gold reward. (3) It sidesteps comparison entirely via `random_group_sample(..., 1)`: when two successful trajectories collapse to the same template/abstract-trajectory, it keeps ONE AT RANDOM rather than judging which routine is better — an explicit refusal to rank. So AWM's "learning" is really CURATION-OF-SUCCESSES, not TRY/COMPARE/REFLECT/INFER. It shows that reinforce-on-success + distill-to-reusable-routine measurably helps agents — a genuine result — but it contributes NOTHING to the oracle-free earned-comparative-judgment question, because it never poses the comparison. For cmk this is the clean adversarial finding: AWM is oracle-BOUND in its headline (offline gold reward) and oracle-DELEGATED in the rest (LLM-judge), and either way it earns "keep this success," never "A beats B." The comparison layer the maintainer is after is precisely the layer AWM omits.
cohort.quote: Paper (online success gate): "We adopt the LM-based evaluation model of Pan et al. (2024) to output a binary label, L_eval(e^t) ∈ {0,1}, that judges if e^t successfully solves q^t by prompting a neural model. If e^t is predicted as success, i.e., 1, we then transform it into workflow(s) I(e^t)→{w^t} and add {w^t} into the agent memory." | Repo README (offline gate): "Switch the criteria to 'gt' (and can skip step 2) if you want to use ground-truth reward as signals to integrate workflows." | Code (induce_rule.py gates): gt → `if json.load(open(.../summary_info.json))["cum_reward"]:` ; autoeval → `if record[0]["rm"]:` ; selection → `random_group_sample(template_dict, 1)` then `random_group_sample(abstraj_dict, 1)` (one-per-group, random — no ranking). | Induction instruction (only "quality" clause = de-dup, not comparison): "Do not generate similar or overlapping workflows. Each workflow should be a commonly-reused sub-routine of the tasks... Try to generate as many workflows that can cover all the tasks in the input list." | Schema: "a textual description of the workflow d; and second, a series of steps to finish the workflow (p1,p2,...)". No failure/negative/pruning path exists in paper or code — only label==1 trajectories are inducted.

cohort.system: ExpeL: LLM Agents Are Experiential Learners (arXiv 2308.10144) — cross-task insight extraction
cohort.memory_shape: An "insight" = a natural-language rule string + an integer "importance count". Operators the LLM applies: ADD / EDIT / UPVOTE / DOWNVOTE. Verbatim: "A newly added insight will have an initial importance count of two associated with it, and the count will increment if subsequent operators UPVOTE or EDIT are applied to it and will decrement when DOWNVOTE is applied. If an insight's importance count reaches zero, it will be removed." No REMOVE op (removal is only the automatic count==0 prune); EDIT counts as an upvote; no max cap stated. The rule content is typically a "do-X-not-Y" contrast. At inference the full list is concatenated into the task spec: ι^ = concat(ι₁,ι₂,ι₃,…).
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: Two extraction modes over an experience pool ℬ that holds ALL training trajectories (both succeed+fail). (1) COMPARE mode: "we let the agent compare a failed trajectory with a successful trajectory for the same task" → the LLM writes a contrast rule (do what the success did, not what the failure did). (2) ALL-SUCCESS mode: "identify patterns within a set of successful trajectories from different tasks" (L-sized chunks). The comparative "A better than B" is thus: oracle labels one trajectory success + one failure → LLM extracts the differentiating rule. The UPVOTE/DOWNVOTE count is cross-pair CORROBORATION: an insight re-derived/agreed-with across many success/fail pairs climbs; a contradicted one auto-prunes at 0. That voting layer robustifies against LLM extraction noise ("even successful trajectories can be suboptimal and mislead the generated insights").
cohort.updates_over_time: Yes — a real learning loop DURING training. As more success/fail pairs are processed, insights accrue UPVOTE/EDIT (count up) or DOWNVOTE (count down), and hit-zero insights are pruned; the insight set revises across the training pass. BUT the loop is train-time only: after training the insight list is frozen and merely concatenated at inference — no revision from inference-time experience (no oracle available there).
cohort.transferable_to_cmk: partial — two separable pieces. (1) NOT transferable: the success/fail labeling that FEEDS the contrast is a benchmark ground-truth oracle ("exact matching for HotpotQA/FEVER, completing in time for ALFWorld, purchasing the matching item for WebShop") — cmk has no such oracle at conversation time, so the earned "A>B" cannot be reproduced as-is. (2) Transferable: the count-gates-survival mechanic (auto-prune at 0) is oracle-free and is the direct fix for the field-wide "inert utility field" anti-pattern (letta Step.feedback, MemOS usefulness_score, cmk's own computed-but-unranked trust_score) — ExpeL is the counter-example where the number is load-bearing. The <situation, do-X-not-Y, why> rule SHAPE is also portable. A cmk adoption would keep ExpeL's insight schema + vote/prune loop but swap the benchmark oracle for the oracle-free passive signals (self-reported outcome, user-correction, cmk-forget) as the success/fail source — and that swap is the entire open problem ExpeL does NOT solve.
cohort.the_hard_part: The earned comparative judgment rests entirely on a GOLD SUCCESS LABEL supplied by the benchmark environment. ExpeL's contrast rules are genuine "A>B" method-knowledge, but they are manufactured by pairing an oracle-confirmed success with an oracle-confirmed failure; strip the oracle and the compare-mode has no way to know which trajectory was better. What it papers over for cmk's use case: it never faces the no-oracle setting. The UPVOTE/DOWNVOTE voting looks like it could substitute for an oracle (peer corroboration of rules), but it corroborates rules OVER pairs that were themselves oracle-labeled — the votes filter LLM extraction noise, they do not generate the success signal. So the honest read: the vote-corroboration + prune-at-0 mechanic is oracle-free and reusable; the judgment it produces is oracle-BOUND and not transferable to a conversation host lacking an answer key.
cohort.quote: "A newly added insight will have an initial importance count of two associated with it, and the count will increment if subsequent operators UPVOTE or EDIT are applied to it and will decrement when DOWNVOTE is applied. If an insight's importance count reaches zero, it will be removed." | "we let the agent compare a failed trajectory with a successful trajectory for the same task" / "identify patterns within a set of successful trajectories from different tasks" | Success defined by the environment: "exact matching for HotpotQA and FEVER, completing the task in time for ALFWorld, and purchasing the item that matches all attributes for WebShop." | "if the agent succeeds, it moves on to the next task. However, if the agent fails, it will look at its failed trajectory and self-reflect." | robustification rationale: "even successful trajectories can be suboptimal and mislead the generated insights."

cohort.system: ReasoningBank (arXiv 2509.25140, Ouyang et al., Google Cloud AI Research + UIUC/Yale; v2 Mar 2026)
cohort.memory_shape: Memory item schema is literally three fields (§3.2, Appendix A.2): "{title, description, content}" — title = "a concise identifier summarizing the core strategy or reasoning pattern"; description = "a brief one-sentence summary"; content = "the distilled reasoning steps, decision rationales, or operational insights." Stored in JSON: "each entry of ReasoningBank consists of a task query, the original trajectory, and the corresponding memory items." Crucially the schema is NOT <win,lose,dimension> — there is no explicit comparative field. The "A>B" judgment is baked into free-text content, in one of two flavors: from a SUCCESS trajectory → "validated strategies" (why-it-worked); from a FAILURE trajectory → "counterfactual pitfalls that act as negative signals" (what-not-to-do + preventive strategy). So a stored item is a <situation-pattern, strategy-or-antistrategy, why> unit, not a head-to-head ranking.
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: TWO distinct mechanisms, with different rigor. (1) BASE ReasoningBank — NOT a direct A-vs-B contrast. Per trajectory, an LLM-as-a-judge emits a binary Success/Failure label ("proxy correctness signals … without any ground-truth reference"), then a success-prompt extracts why-it-worked strategies and a failure-prompt (separate template, Fig 9) reflects "why the trajectory failed" and extracts preventive lessons. The "better-than" here is DEFERRED and implicit: both good strategies and pitfalls land in one pool, and at retrieval the agent is told to "explicitly discuss if you want to use each memory item or not" — the comparison happens later in-context, not at write time. (2) MaTTS-PARALLEL self-contrast (Fig 11) — THIS is the real earned comparative judgment: generate k trajectories for the SAME query (some succeed, some fail), feed ALL of them at once, and instruct "Why did some trajectories succeed while others failed? … Identify patterns and strategies that consistently led to success [and] mistakes … from failed trajectories." The paper is explicit this is contrast, not judging: "Instead of relying on the LLM to act as an external judge of quality, the model is guided to directly compare and contrast trajectories … This provides a contrastive signal that grounds the memory extraction process in observable differences between outcomes." That last clause is the mechanic — A>B is inferred from OBSERVABLE OUTCOME DIFFERENCES across sibling runs, not from a score.
cohort.updates_over_time: One-shot distilled then FROZEN — this is the paper's biggest honesty gap for a learning-loop. Consolidation is deliberately add-only: "we adopt a minimal consolidation strategy: newly generated items are directly added without additional pruning … Nevertheless, more advanced consolidation mechanisms (e.g., merging, forgetting) can be incorporated in future work." So a judgment is never revised, down-ranked, or retracted as later episodes contradict it — the pool only grows. There IS a system-level feedback loop (better memory → better exploration → better memory, the "positive feedback loop"), but no per-item revision loop. A wrong "A>B" written once stays in the pool forever; the only correction is a NEWER item eventually out-competing it at retrieval by embedding similarity. Contrast with Memento/case-bank approaches that re-weight — RB does not.
cohort.the_hard_part: The honest crux: RB earns "A>B" oracle-FREE at memory-formation time, but it buys that in a setting cmk does not have — REPLAYABILITY. The whole engine assumes you can run the SAME task k times (MaTTS parallel scaling, k trajectories per query) so that success and failure are OBSERVED SIDE-BY-SIDE on identical inputs, which is what makes the contrast valid ("observable differences between outcomes"). That is a benchmark/agent-harness luxury: a resettable environment + a task you can re-attempt. cmk observes ONE real, non-repeatable conversation — there is no sibling failed trajectory to contrast the success against, so the clean self-contrast signal is unavailable; cmk would fall back to RB's WEAKER base path (the LLM-as-judge binary label on a single trajectory), which is exactly the part with only 72.7% raw agreement with truth. Second gap: even the base label needs a checkable outcome ("did the final web state match intent"); a conversation's "did this advice actually work" is often only knowable episodes later (or never), and RB has no mechanism to bind a judgment to a delayed outcome. Third: what it papers over — (a) the judge-robustness ablation (SR flat from 70–90% judge accuracy, Fig 8) proves robustness to NOISE but was run on WebArena where a real gold label EXISTS to simulate against; it does not prove the loop works where no gold label exists at all, only that the loop tolerates a noisy proxy of one. (b) Add-only consolidation means the "learning" never unlearns — no revision loop. (c) The Best-of-N "select the single best trajectory" prompt (Fig 12) DOES rank trajectories head-to-head, but that path is used for BoN answer-selection scoring, not fed into memory — the memory path uses the softer self-contrast, so the sharpest A>B artifact in the paper is not the one that gets stored. Net for cmk: RB shows oracle-free comparative judgment IS possible and even robust to a noisy self-judge — but its strong version rides on same-task replay cmk lacks, and its stored form is a frozen free-text lesson, not a revisable ranking.
cohort.quote: "we adopt an LLM-as-a-judge to label outcomes as success or failure given the query and trajectory without any ground-truth reference. Based on these signals … successful experiences contribute validated strategies, while failed ones supply counterfactual signals and pitfalls" (§3.2). And the self-contrast core: "Instead of relying on the LLM to act as an external judge of quality, the model is guided to directly compare and contrast trajectories, identifying patterns that lead to success and mistakes that cause failure. This provides a contrastive signal that grounds the memory extraction process in observable differences between outcomes" (Appendix A.3). Oracle boundary: judge "baseline accuracy by comparing the judge's predictions against ground-truth labels, which we find to be 72.7% … the judge's accuracy does not significantly impact the performance of ReasoningBank" (§4) — ground-truth appears ONLY in eval: "A task is marked as 'successful' only if the agent's final output or state precisely matches the pre-defined ground-truth goal" (Appendix B.1, Evaluation Metrics — reporting only, not memory formation).

cohort.system: Voyager (MineDojo/Voyager, arXiv 2305.16291) — LLM lifelong-learning Minecraft agent with an ever-growing skill library
cohort.memory_shape: Each library entry is a passed-verification skill, NOT a comparative judgment. Stored shape (voyager/agents/skill.py): {"code": program_code, "description": skill_description, plus a Chroma vector embedding}. add_texts(texts=[skill_description], ids=[program_name], metadatas=[{"name": program_name}]). Retrieval key = embedding of an LLM-generated <=6-sentence natural-language summary of the function ("Try to summarize the function in no more than 6 sentences. Your response should be a single line of text"). There is NO field for "why A>B", no win/lose pair, no dimension, no rank, no counterfactual. The schema literally cannot express "method A beat method B" — it only records "here is a program that once passed."
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: It does NOT earn "A better than B" — this is the honest, adversarial finding. It earns a weaker, single-skill binary: "this ONE program completed its task." Mechanics (all code-verified): the action agent writes a JS program; a self-verification "critic" agent (critic.py, check_task_success) renders the agent's post-execution game state (biome/health/inventory/position/nearby-blocks) into text and asks an LLM to return {success:bool, critique:str} against the task text ("You are required to evaluate if I have met the task requirements. Exceeding the task requirements is also considered a success while failing to meet them requires you to provide critique to help me improve"). The iterative loop runs up to action_agent_task_max_retries=4 rounds, feeding execution-errors + the critic's critique back for revision. Retention gate: `if info["success"]: self.skill_manager.add_new_skill(info)` — a skill enters the library IFF the critic said success; failed programs are DISCARDED, never stored. There is zero comparison between two competing skills; the library keeps the FIRST program that passes. The nearest thing to revision is name-collision archiving (old skill -> V2/V3 file, vectordb entry deleted+re-added under the same name) — but it overwrites UNCONDITIONALLY, with no old-vs-new quality comparison, so a worse re-implementation silently replaces a better one.
cohort.updates_over_time: One-shot per skill, then effectively frozen. A skill is added once (on the verification pass) and never re-scored against later experience. The curriculum tracks completed_tasks/failed_tasks but NEVER ranks skills, NEVER deprecates a skill because a better one appeared, and NEVER retries a failed task with an upgraded strategy ("Once a task fails, it remains in the failed list permanently"; failed tasks are just marked "too hard" to avoid re-assignment). No learning LOOP revises a stored judgment as evidence accumulates — the library is monotonic accumulate-what-passed. The only mutation is unconditional overwrite on name collision, which is not evidence-driven revision.
cohort.transferable_to_cmk: partial. Transferable idea: the prune-on-failure asymmetry (discard non-passing artifacts; only reinforce what verified) + storing a reusable UNIT keyed by an LLM-written natural-language summary embedding — cmk could store "recipes/skills" keyed by a summary and retrieve by similarity. NOT transferable: (1) the whole better-than question is UNANSWERED here — Voyager never compares two skills, so it offers no mechanism for earning comparative method-judgment; (2) its verification leans on a grounded, machine-inspectable world state (inventory counts, hunger==20.0) that a game hands you for free — cmk's conversation domain has no equivalent inspectable ground truth, so cmk's "did this method work?" is strictly harder and more oracle-poor than Voyager's; (3) unconditional overwrite is an anti-pattern cmk should NOT copy (a worse fact silently clobbering a better one).
cohort.quote: skill.py: `self.vectordb.add_texts(texts=[skill_description], ids=[program_name], metadatas=[{"name": program_name}])` and retrieval `docs_and_scores = self.vectordb.similarity_search_with_score(query, k=k)`. voyager.py retention gate: `if info["success"]: self.skill_manager.add_new_skill(info)`; retry loop `done = (self.action_agent_rollout_num_iter >= self.action_agent_task_max_retries or success)` (default max_retries=4). critic.txt: "You are required to evaluate if I have met the task requirements. Exceeding the task requirements is also considered a success while failing to meet them requires you to provide critique to help me improve" (e.g. "You have enough materials to craft a wooden pickaxe, but you didn't craft it" -> success:false). skill.txt: "Try to summarize the function in no more than 6 sentences. Your response should be a single line of text."
cohort.the_hard_part: The crux Voyager exposes for cmk: Voyager NEVER earns "A is better than B" — it only earns "this single program passed once," a binary per-episode verdict, then appends it monotonically. What it papers over: (a) NO cross-skill competition — two skills that solve the same task never race; the first passer wins by default and the loser is never even compared, so "better" is decided by arrival order, not merit; (b) the self-verifier is an LLM-judge with no appeal — it can hallucinate success (it interprets rendered state, and a false success:true permanently injects a bad skill); (c) unconditional name-collision overwrite means a REGRESSION (worse V3 replacing better V2) is invisible because nothing scores old-vs-new. The reason Voyager can get away with binary-not-comparative is that Minecraft gives an inspectable, unambiguous world-state oracle (item counts, tech-tree tiers) that makes "did it work?" cheap — cmk has NO such oracle at conversation time, so cmk cannot even borrow Voyager's binary verifier, let alone find a comparative one here. The valuable finding is negative: the canonical skill-accumulation agent solves KEEP-what-verified, and deliberately does NOT solve rank-what's-better — that problem is left entirely open.

cohort.system: GEPA (Genetic-Pareto) — reflective prompt/text evolution optimizer (arXiv 2507.19457; ref impl github.com/gepa-ai/gepa)
cohort.memory_shape: GEPA has no fact store; its "memory" is a pool of candidate programs plus a per-instance score matrix. The load-bearing structures (from code): (a) EvaluationBatch{outputs: list[RolloutOutput], scores: list[float], trajectories: list[Trajectory]|None, objective_scores: list[dict[str,float]]|None} in core/adapter.py — the scalar record of one candidate's rollout; (b) state.per_program_tracked_scores (per-candidate, per-instance floats) + get_pareto_front_mapping() (per training instance → set of candidates that hit the max score there) in strategies/candidate_selector.py — this IS the stored "judgment," but it is a NUMERIC dominance matrix, not a natural-language "A beats B because…" record; (c) make_reflective_dataset() → a component-keyed Mapping[str, Sequence[Mapping]] pairing failed rollouts with their diagnostic traces, fed to the reflection LM. The NL reflection ("this failed because the prompt didn't handle X") is generated and consumed transiently to WRITE the next prompt — it is NOT persisted as a durable comparative-method judgment. So the only durable "A>B" is the scalar Pareto matrix.
cohort.how_it_earns_better_than: Two-stage, and the winner is decided by SCALAR SCORE, not by the reflection. (1) MUTATION (NL, oracle-free-ish): reflection LM reads full traces — reasoning, tool calls, tool outputs, error messages, profiler output, and any evaluator "feedback string" (what GEPA calls Actionable Side Information / the feedback function μ_f) — and PROPOSES a new prompt. This diagnoses "why B failed" in language, richer than a scalar gradient. But it only writes a candidate; it does not adjudicate. (2) SELECTION (scalar, the actual "better than"): the new candidate is rolled out on a minibatch and accepted only if StrictImprovementAcceptance passes — literally new_sum = sum(subsample_scores_after) > old_sum = sum(subsample_scores_before) (core/engine.py). If accepted it's scored on the validation set; get_pareto_front_mapping() then records, per instance, which candidate achieved the max float. The next parent to mutate is sampled with probability proportional to how many instances it is Pareto-best on. So "A is better than B" = "A's metric μ returned a higher float on those instances." Reflection is the mutation operator; μ is the sole selection operator. The natural-language "reflection" never earns the comparative verdict — it's earned by the numeric metric every time.
cohort.updates_over_time: Yes — it is a real iterative loop, but over CANDIDATES not over a distilled judgment. Each round: select Pareto parent → reflect+mutate → minibatch accept-gate → full-eval → update the Pareto score matrix → repeat. The dominance matrix revises as new candidates arrive (a candidate that wins new instances joins the frontier; strictly-dominated candidates are pruned). But this is optimization convergence within ONE run against a FIXED metric, not cross-episode learning of a transferable "in situation X, method Y beats Z" rule. When the run ends you keep the best prompt text, not a reusable why-A-beats-B memory. The comparative signal is one-shot per candidate (its μ score) and frozen into the matrix; the reflection rationale is discarded after it has done its mutation job.
cohort.transferable_to_cmk: partial — and the transferable part is exactly the part GEPA doesn't itself keep. Directly reusable for cmk: (1) the SPLIT — separate the "propose a better approach" step (LLM reflection over traces, oracle-free) from the "confirm it's actually better" step. cmk can adopt the reflection-as-mutation idea (write a candidate convention/strategy from a failure trace) with NO oracle. (2) Pareto-over-subsets instead of a single scalar — store which strategy "won" on which KIND of situation rather than one global ranking, so complementary strategies survive (maps well to cmk's per-topic facts). NOT transferable without an oracle: GEPA's actual "better than" is always a per-instance FLOAT the environment/metric hands back; cmk at conversation time has no such re-scorable metric and cannot re-run a minibatch. So cmk could borrow GEPA's reflection-writes-the-candidate mechanic, but would have to replace GEPA's scalar-μ acceptance gate with cmk's own oracle-free outcome signals (the settled portfolio) — GEPA gives no oracle-free acceptance test, only an oracle-free PROPOSAL step.
cohort.the_hard_part: The honest crux: GEPA does NOT solve oracle-free comparative judgment — it relocates the oracle and papers over that with "if you can measure it, you can optimize it." The architecture is oracle-AGNOSTIC (adapter.evaluate(batch, candidate) receives zero gold labels from the core; the adapter decides scoring), which reads as oracle-free — but every actual "A>B" decision consumes a scalar the metric must produce, and that scalar is a ground-truth-quality signal: in the paper's headline benchmarks (AIME/HotpotQA/MATH) μ is literally a gold-answer key (DSPy signature metric(gold, pred, ...) — gold is a required arg), and in the "oracle-free" cases (cloud-scheduling cost −40%, kernel runtime, code-agent resolve-rate) μ is a directly OBSERVED outcome (cost, latency, compile/pass) that the environment measures cheaply and repeatably. Both require that you can RE-RUN a candidate and get a fresh trustworthy number. GEPA never earns "A is better than B" from pure passive cross-episode observation with no re-scorable signal — the exact situation cmk is in at conversation time (single trajectory, no re-run, no metric). Its genuinely oracle-free contribution is narrower than it looks: reflection makes the PROPOSAL step label-free and richer than a gradient, but the ADJUDICATION step is a hard scalar every time. The richness of the NL "why" is used and then thrown away; what persists as the durable judgment is just a float matrix.
cohort.quote: core/engine.py acceptance gate: StrictImprovementAcceptance → "New subsample score {new_sum} is better than old score {old_sum}", where new_sum = sum(proposal.subsample_scores_after or []) and old_sum = sum(proposal.subsample_scores_before or []). core/adapter.py: EvaluationBatch{outputs: list[RolloutOutput]; scores: list[float]; trajectories: list[Trajectory] | None; objective_scores: list[dict[str,float]] | None}; def evaluate(self, batch: list[DataInst], candidate: dict[str,str], capture_traces: bool=False) -> EvaluationBatch — NO gold arg; docstring "If an example fails (e.g., parse error), use a fallback score (e.g., 0.0)." DSPy binding: def metric(gold: Example, pred: Prediction, trace=None, pred_name=None, pred_trace=None) -> float | ScoreWithFeedback — gold is required; feedback default = f'This trajectory got a score of {score}.' Abstract: GEPA "reflects on them in natural language to diagnose problems, propose and test prompt updates, and combine complementary lessons from the Pareto frontier of its own attempts." README: ASI = "diagnostic feedback returned by evaluators that serves as the text-optimization analogue of a gradient." Pareto: select_program_candidate_from_pareto_front(state.get_pareto_front_mapping(), state.per_program_tracked_scores, self.rng) — sample proportional to instance-coverage.

lenses.lens: Preference learning / RLHF-RLAIF (DPO + Bradley-Terry, LLM-as-judge pairwise, RLAIF/Constitutional-AI, topological consistency)
lenses.how_better_than_is_inferred_without_orac:
This field's entire premise is oracle-free relative judgment, so it is the right lens — but the mechanics only work by REPLACING the oracle with three substitutes, none of which a single-user session has for free:

1. PAIRWISE > POINTWISE (the core move). Nobody scores A on an absolute scale. You show a judge A and B together and ask "which is better?" Grounding each item in the other removes the absolute-calibration burden. Verbatim (eugeneyan/LLM-evaluators): "pairwise comparisons lead to more stable results and smaller differences between LLM judgments and human annotations relative to direct scoring." Crucial asymmetry FOR THE KIT: this only beats pointwise for SUBJECTIVE dimensions ("tone", "coherence", "which method felt cleaner"). For OBJECTIVE ones the same source says direct scoring wins — "a response is either faithful to the provided context or it is not." So "method A > method B" splits: if the difference is objective (A's tests passed, B's didn't) you don't need this machinery at all (that IS the kit's tool-result signal); if it's subjective, pairwise is the only reliable form but it inherits every judge bias below.

2. BRADLEY-TERRY: turn a PILE of noisy pairwise preferences into a latent scalar STRENGTH per item. P(A>B) = exp(r_A)/(exp(r_A)+exp(r_B)). You never observe r; you FIT it by maximum-likelihood so the recovered strengths best explain all the observed win/loss pairs. This is the actual "how A earns > B without a score": the score is INFERRED from a graph of many overlapping comparisons, not measured. DPO's insight is you don't even need the explicit r — the implicit reward r(x,y)=β·log(π(y|x)/π_ref(y|x)) is recoverable from the policy itself; the Bradley-Terry partition function Z(x) cancels when you subtract chosen-minus-rejected, so only the RELATIVE ordering within a pair is ever used.

3. TRANSITIVITY AS A DENOISING VOTE (TCR, arXiv 2510.15514) — the single most transferable mechanic. A lone pairwise verdict is noise-prone; a GRAPH of them self-corrects. Verbatim: "transitivity serves as a denoising mechanism: systematic signals A→B and B→C structurally 'vote' for A→C, while random errors cluster into topologically exposed cycles." Cycles (A≻B≻C≻A) are treated as measurement error, not real intransitivity, and "edges participating in more cycles are systematically more likely to be erroneous" — so you delete the edges that break the most cycles (approx. Maximum Acyclic Subgraph) and the surviving order IS the earned ranking. Ground-truth is replaced by INTERNAL CONSISTENCY across many judgments.

4. THE REFERENCE ANCHOR (DPO's π_ref / RLHF's KL term). "Better-than" is never absolute — it is always better THAN A BASELINE. Remove the reference anchor and the model "completely degenerates." Preference learning cannot judge "is A good?", only "is A better than the reference?" — every earned judgment is relative to a held-fixed comparison point.

5. RLAIF/Constitutional-AI: replace the human labeler with an LLM judging against a WRITTEN PRINCIPLE. Verbatim (Bai 2022, arXiv 2212.08073): given a response pair "the model is queried via a multiple-choice prompt to identify the less harmful response," and "the normalized log-probabilities over the answer choices are then used as soft preference targets." The principle IS the rubric; the "oracle" is a natural-language rule the system already holds, not an answer key. Soft (probabilistic) labels, not hard 0/1.
lenses.what_it_requires:
SCALE and BOTH-ARMS-OBSERVED — and this is where the lens partially breaks for a single-user kit. Preconditions:

(a) BOTH ARMS OBSERVED PER COMPARISON. Bradley-Terry/DPO need (prompt, chosen, rejected) — you must see A AND B for the SAME situation. A session almost never runs method B counterfactually; it runs A, and B is the road not taken. This is the fundamental mismatch: the kit observes ONE arm per episode, preference learning needs TWO. (The kit's memory notes already flag "counterfactual ΔR" as a signal — this lens explains WHY it's hard: without the B-arm you have no pair.)

(b) MANY OVERLAPPING COMPARISONS PER ITEM. TCR standardizes G=4 candidates and evaluates all (G choose 2) pairs, and its theory needs "sufficient scale n≥4 (preferably n≥8 for strong concentration)." Bradley-Terry strength estimates are only stable when items share many overlapping opponents. A single user producing a handful of method-choices per project has n≈1–3 comparisons per method — squarely in TCR's named failure regime ("At boundary cases (n=3 or p≈0.5), discrimination between random error and systematic signal edges weakens"). Industrial preference datasets are ~10^5 pairs (Gao's overoptimization curves index ~150K samples); a single user generates maybe dozens of durable method-verdicts per YEAR.

(c) A JUDGE THAT IS DISINTERESTED. Pairwise judging needs a rater whose biases don't correlate with the thing judged. In a single-user kit the only available judge is the SAME model that produced the method — the self-preference problem (below).

CAN A SINGLE SESSION MEET THESE? No, not the scale ones. It CAN meet: pairwise framing (whenever the user actually tried A-then-B on the same task — rare but high-value), the reference-anchor idea (judge every method against a fixed baseline, not in the abstract), and transitivity-consistency across the FEW comparisons that accumulate over many sessions (the kit is the cross-session substrate, so its denominator grows with project lifetime, not per-session).
lenses.failure_modes:
The adversarial core — every mechanic above has a documented way it manufactures a FALSE "A>B":

1. JUDGE BIASES with measured magnitudes (eugeneyan, verbatim) — these are large, not marginal. POSITION BIAS: "gpt-3.5 being biased 50% of the time and claude-v1 being biased 70% of the time" toward the first-shown option. VERBOSITY BIAS: "both claude-v1 and gpt-3.5 preferred the longer response more than 90% of the time" — a memory kit that judges "the more detailed method note is better" is measuring length, not quality. SELF-PREFERENCE: "gpt-4 favored itself with a 10% higher win rate while claude-v1 favored itself with a 25% higher win rate" — fatal for a single-user kit whose judge and author are the same model: it will systematically rate ITS OWN past method higher, a self-confirming loop. Mitigation (order-swap + aggregate, fairness prompts) requires running the comparison BOTH ways — doable but doubles cost.

2. REWARD HACKING / GOODHART (Gao 2023, scaling-laws for overoptimization). "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." Once the kit RANKS memories by a learned "which-method-won" proxy and then optimizes behavior toward high-proxy methods, "performance as measured by the learned proxy reward increases, but true quality plateaus or even deteriorates." And it degrades PREDICTABLY with distance from baseline (KL) — the further the kit's learned preferences drift from its starting priors, the less trustworthy they get. Directly relevant to ADR-0017's "rank retrieval by trust blend": the moment trust_score becomes a ranking target, it's Goodhart-exposed.

3. SMALL-SAMPLE INTRANSITIVITY MISREAD AS SIGNAL. TCR assumes cycles = noise. But it explicitly warns "When genuine intransitivity dominates, TCR may incorrectly filter valid preference signals" — and method choice IS genuinely multi-criteria (A is faster, B is simpler, C is more portable; no total order exists). A single user's method preferences are often legitimately context-dependent; forcing them into one ranking DESTROYS the real signal. At n=3 the denoiser can't tell a real cycle from a noisy one.

4. SELF-CONFIRMATION / CIRCULARITY (the RLAIF self-judge trap). "Using a model as its own judge can work surprisingly well for bootstrapping, but it also risks circularity... The literature shows repeated failures when teams treat the synthetic evaluator as ground truth." A single-user kit has NO external judge — so any "A>B" it earns is the model agreeing with itself, and errors compound rather than cancel. This is the honest ceiling: without a second independent rater (another model, or the user), oracle-free "better-than" in a single-user kit is self-referential.

5. SOFT-LABEL COLLAPSE at low signal: CAI uses log-prob soft targets precisely because hard labels overfit small data. A kit that records hard "A beat B" from one episode over-commits to noise.
lenses.transferable_kernel:
ONE kernel transfers, and it is NOT the Bradley-Terry/DPO machinery (that needs scale + both-arms-observed the kit lacks). The kernel is: **"better-than" is earned by CONSISTENCY ACROSS MANY RELATIVE COMPARISONS AGAINST A FIXED REFERENCE — never by one absolute judgment — and a comparison graph SELF-CORRECTS its own noise via transitivity, letting the kit down-weight verdicts that contradict the accumulated web without ever needing a ground-truth score.**

Concretely, for a single-user oracle-free memory kit:

(a) STORE METHOD JUDGMENTS AS PAIRWISE + REFERENCED, NEVER ABSOLUTE. Not "method A is good (trust 0.8)" but "for task-shape T, A was preferred over B, because <reason>, on <date>." The pair + the fixed reference (B, or 'the previous approach') is what carries earned meaning. This directly upgrades cmk from storing FACTS to storing the JUDGMENT-shaped record the maintainer asked for, and it's the DPO reference-anchor insight applied to memory: every method-verdict is relative to a named baseline.

(b) LET CONSISTENCY, NOT COUNT, BE THE STRENGTH SIGNAL (the TCR kernel, downgraded to the kit's tiny-n regime). Across sessions, if A>B and B>C both recur and nothing says C>A, the web is coherent → the ordering has earned trust. If a new episode says C>A, you have a CYCLE = a contradiction signal (which the kit already computes for facts!) — treat the method-preference cycle exactly like a fact-contradiction: DON'T pick a winner, FLAG it to the human, because at single-user scale a cycle is more likely genuine multi-criteria intransitivity than noise. This is the honest inversion of TCR: TCR (at scale) deletes cycle edges as noise; the kit (at n≈2) surfaces cycles as "these methods are context-dependent — ask the user which criterion matters." Transitivity becomes a DETECTOR of "there is no single better", which is itself the valuable earned judgment.

(c) HARD-CAP THE SELF-JUDGE. Because the kit's only rater is the same model (self-preference bias 10–25%, self-confirmation circularity), it must NEVER treat a model-generated "A>B" as settled — only as a proposal. The one rater that IS independent and oracle-grade is the USER; so the kit's earned-judgment loop should PRUNE on the disinterested signals it already has (user-correction, cmk-forget, tool-result, a re-ask) and only PROPOSE preferences from self-comparison, never auto-commit them to ranking. This is the same prune-on-failure > reinforce-on-success asymmetry the base already settled, re-derived from a different field: RLHF confirms reinforce-on-success needs scale + an oracle the kit lacks, while the disinterested failure signals need neither.

HONEST BOTTOM LINE: preference learning proves oracle-free "better-than" is REAL and mechanizable — but every working instance BUYS it with scale (10^4–10^5 comparisons), both-arms-observed pairs, order-swap debiasing, and a KL anchor against overoptimization. A single-user kit has none of the scale. So the transferable kernel is the SHAPE (pairwise-against-a-reference + consistency-as-trust + cycle-as-contradiction-flag + self-judge-is-only-a-proposal), NOT the estimator. The kit can earn "A>B" the way RLHF does in KIND but not in CONFIDENCE — and the intellectually honest design consequence is to render most method-verdicts as human-flagged proposals and reserve hard auto-updates for the disinterested failure signals, exactly matching ADR-0017's prune-asymmetry.

lenses.lens: Human / cognitive-science of skill: how experts earn "method A > method B" without a benchmark (Ericsson deliberate practice; Schön reflective practice; Army After-Action Review / internal-prediction error; case-based reasoning; expertise & metacognition research).
lenses.how_better_than_is_inferred_without_orac:
One mechanism recurs across all four traditions, and it is genuinely oracle-free: **the learner sets an EXPECTATION, then treats the gap between expected and observed as the signal.** The reference is self-GENERATED, not externally supplied — that is the whole trick. Concretely:

1. INTERNAL-PREDICTION ERROR (the load-bearing kernel). Ericsson: the expert "gradually acquires mechanisms that increase their ability to control, self-monitor, and evaluate their performance... and thus gain INDEPENDENCE from the feedback of their teachers."
